narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile shows some solid strengths, like your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which stands out above the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 48% chance that you would fail it—high enough to trigger a rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to succeed right away. This decision hinged on just a few key factors from your academic record that outweighed your other qualifications. The most influential was your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1st, which is much lower than the average 6th decile; this strongly signaled to the model a higher risk of bar exam struggles, pushing the prediction toward failure. Close behind was your Year 3 decile of 2nd, again well below the typical 6th decile, reinforcing that early and mid-undergrad performance dip as a major concern. On a brighter note, your above-average undergrad GPA pulled in the opposite direction, helping to argue against failure and softening the overall prediction somewhat. Compared to typical students, your LSAT score of 31 falls short of the 37 average, but it wasn't the top driver here—your school deciles carried more weight in the model's eyes. While factors like your full-time status (common for 93% of students) and family income didn't sway things much, the model focused on those early grades as the biggest red flags for long-term bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man from a supportive background—it's the algorithm weighing patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it toward the average, strengthening your undergrad record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying to schools with more holistic reviews that might value your unique story. You could also target programs with strong bar prep support tailored for diverse students like yourself. Keep pushing—many overcome similar hurdles and thrive in law.
